raw_response: {"rating": -2.5, "lean": "Lean Left", "explanation": "This is a news report. The author’s own prose consistently frames the EPA’s website edits and pending rule repeal under the Trump administration as suppressing established climate science and undermining federal climate action, while highlighting harms from removed resources. The accumulation of evaluative phrasing and selective emphasis supports a Lean Left rating of -2.5. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author characterizes the administration’s energy agenda with charged verbs — “tries to supercharge US oil and gas production and resurrect the coal industry” — signaling disapproval of expanded fossil fuel use; (2) The piece states, in the author’s voice, that burning oil, gas and coal are “the key drivers of a warming climate since the industrial revolution,” then contrasts this with EPA’s removal of such references, implying excision of factual consensus; (3) The author asserts the repeal would “effectively take away the federal government’s main way to fight climate change,” a normative framing that presumes the desirability of robust federal climate regulation; (4) The endangerment finding is labeled a “landmark scientific finding,” an evaluative endorsement of the regulatory basis; (5) The author writes, “It’s not the first time the Trump administration has downplayed climate change,” directly ascribing a pattern of minimization to the administration; (6) The DOE report authors are described as “a small group of longtime climate contrarians” who “minimized humans’ role,” a dismissive characterization that signals alignment against their viewpoint; (7) Structural/source selection: the article platforms multiple climate scientists and a former Biden EPA official criticizing the changes, with only brief inclusion of the agency’s defense, and no independent experts supporting the revisions, indicating a one-sided evidentiary tilt; (8) The removal of EPA “climate change indicators” is framed as harmful by noting it “was a popular resource among educators,” emphasizing public loss; (9) The piece includes procedural and factual context (timelines, rule basis, affected sectors), which modestly tempers the overall lean but does not offset the consistent critical framing."}

parsed_output.explanation: This is a news report. The author’s own prose consistently frames the EPA’s website edits and pending rule repeal under the Trump administration as suppressing established climate science and undermining federal climate action, while highlighting harms from removed resources. The accumulation of evaluative phrasing and selective emphasis supports a Lean Left rating of -2.5. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author characterizes the administration’s energy agenda with charged verbs — “tries to supercharge US oil and gas production and resurrect the coal industry” — signaling disapproval of expanded fossil fuel use; (2) The piece states, in the author’s voice, that burning oil, gas and coal are “the key drivers of a warming climate since the industrial revolution,” then contrasts this with EPA’s removal of such references, implying excision of factual consensus; (3) The author asserts the repeal would “effectively take away the federal government’s main way to fight climate change,” a normative framing that presumes the desirability of robust federal climate regulation; (4) The endangerment finding is labeled a “landmark scientific finding,” an evaluative endorsement of the regulatory basis; (5) The author writes, “It’s not the first time the Trump administration has downplayed climate change,” directly ascribing a pattern of minimization to the administration; (6) The DOE report authors are described as “a small group of longtime climate contrarians” who “minimized humans’ role,” a dismissive characterization that signals alignment against their viewpoint; (7) Structural/source selection: the article platforms multiple climate scientists and a former Biden EPA official criticizing the changes, with only brief inclusion of the agency’s defense, and no independent experts supporting the revisions, indicating a one-sided evidentiary tilt; (8) The removal of EPA “climate change indicators” is framed as harmful by noting it “was a popular resource among educators,” emphasizing public loss; (9) The piece includes procedural and factual context (timelines, rule basis, affected sectors), which modestly tempers the overall lean but does not offset the consistent critical framing.

explanation: This is a news report. The author’s own prose consistently frames the EPA’s website edits and pending rule repeal under the Trump administration as suppressing established climate science and undermining federal climate action, while highlighting harms from removed resources. The accumulation of evaluative phrasing and selective emphasis supports a Lean Left rating of -2.5. Detailed Analysis: (1) The author characterizes the administration’s energy agenda with charged verbs — “tries to supercharge US oil and gas production and resurrect the coal industry” — signaling disapproval of expanded fossil fuel use; (2) The piece states, in the author’s voice, that burning oil, gas and coal are “the key drivers of a warming climate since the industrial revolution,” then contrasts this with EPA’s removal of such references, implying excision of factual consensus; (3) The author asserts the repeal would “effectively take away the federal government’s main way to fight climate change,” a normative framing that presumes the desirability of robust federal climate regulation; (4) The endangerment finding is labeled a “landmark scientific finding,” an evaluative endorsement of the regulatory basis; (5) The author writes, “It’s not the first time the Trump administration has downplayed climate change,” directly ascribing a pattern of minimization to the administration; (6) The DOE report authors are described as “a small group of longtime climate contrarians” who “minimized humans’ role,” a dismissive characterization that signals alignment against their viewpoint; (7) Structural/source selection: the article platforms multiple climate scientists and a former Biden EPA official criticizing the changes, with only brief inclusion of the agency’s defense, and no independent experts supporting the revisions, indicating a one-sided evidentiary tilt; (8) The removal of EPA “climate change indicators” is framed as harmful by noting it “was a popular resource among educators,” emphasizing public loss; (9) The piece includes procedural and factual context (timelines, rule basis, affected sectors), which modestly tempers the overall lean but does not offset the consistent critical framing.
